response:
The error you're encountering is due to a mismatch between the version of the .NET runtime and the dependencies your WPF application is expecting. When running on Windows, your application is trying to load a specific version of the PresentationFramework assembly (version 4.0.0.0), which is not available in .NET 7. This is a common issue when mixing different .NET versions and frameworks. The WPF framework is tightly coupled with .NET Framework and is not fully supported in .NET Core or .NET 7, which is why you're seeing the file not found error. To resolve this, you need to ensure that the correct version of the WPF assemblies is available when the application runs on Windows.

To address this, you can consider using a multi-targeting approach where your main application is compiled for both .NET 7 and .NET Framework. This allows you to conditionally include the necessary WPF assemblies when targeting Windows and exclude them when targeting macOS. However, this approach may complicate your build process and could lead to larger application sizes. Alternatively, you can use a tool like ILMerge or a more modern solution like .NET Native to bundle the required WPF assemblies into your application. This ensures that the correct version of the PresentationFramework is available at runtime without requiring the user to have it installed separately.

Cousin to this, you could also consider using a separate WPF project that is only built and deployed when targeting Windows. This way, the main application remains lightweight and focused on cross-platform functionality, while the WPF-specific code is isolated in its own project. This approach allows you to manage dependencies more effectively and ensures that the WPF assemblies are only included when necessary. You can then use conditional compilation or build configurations to control which project is included in the final output based on the target platform. This method helps keep your main application clean and avoids unnecessary dependencies.

Another option is to use a dependency injection approach where the WPF components are loaded dynamically at runtime. This allows your main application to remain platform-agnostic, and the WPF UI can be loaded only when the application is running on Windows. You can use reflection or a custom loader to detect the platform and load the appropriate assemblies. This method requires careful management of the application's startup process and may introduce additional complexity, but it offers greater flexibility and maintainability in the long run. It also ensures that the WPF assemblies are not included in the macOS build, preventing the file not found error from occurring.
